exercises.title: Trace fixed-size sliding window
exercises.instructions: Trace maxSumWindow([3,1,2,5,8,2,1,4,3,6], k=3). Show each window and sum.
exercises.expected_solution:
Array: [3,1,2,5,8,2,1,4,3,6], k=3

Window [0..2]: 3+1+2=6
Window [1..3]: 6-3+5=8   (slide: remove 3, add 5)
Window [2..4]: 8-1+8=15  (slide: remove 1, add 8)
Window [3..5]: 15-2+2=15 (slide: remove 2, add 2)
Window [4..6]: 15-5+1=11 (slide: remove 5, add 1)
Window [5..7]: 11-8+4=7  (slide: remove 8, add 4)
Window [6..8]: 7-2+3=8   (slide: remove 2, add 3)
Window [7..9]: 8-1+6=13  (slide: remove 1, add 6)

MAX = 15 (window [2..4]: [2,5,8] or [3..5]: [5,8,2])

Slide formula: newSum = oldSum - arr[hi-k] + arr[hi]
O(n) total: each element added once, removed once

test_cases.expected:
Array: [3,1,2,5,8,2,1,4,3,6], k=3

Window [0..2]: 3+1+2=6
Window [1..3]: 6-3+5=8   (slide: remove 3, add 5)
Window [2..4]: 8-1+8=15  (slide: remove 1, add 8)
Window [3..5]: 15-2+2=15 (slide: remove 2, add 2)
Window [4..6]: 15-5+1=11 (slide: remove 5, add 1)
Window [5..7]: 11-8+4=7  (slide: remove 8, add 4)
Window [6..8]: 7-2+3=8   (slide: remove 2, add 3)
Window [7..9]: 8-1+6=13  (slide: remove 1, add 6)

MAX = 15 (window [2..4]: [2,5,8] or [3..5]: [5,8,2])

Slide formula: newSum = oldSum - arr[hi-k] + arr[hi]
O(n) total: each element added once, removed once

hints: Slide: newSum = oldSum - leftElem + rightElem
Initial window: sum of first k elements
Iterate hi from k to n-1

exercises.title: Fixed vs variable window — which to use?
exercises.instructions:
State fixed window (F), variable window (V), or neither (N):
A: Maximum sum of exactly k consecutive elements
B: Longest substring without repeating characters
C: Whether array contains duplicate within k positions of each other
D: Minimum length subarray with sum ≥ target
E: Sort the array
exercises.expected_solution:
A: Max sum exactly k consecutive → FIXED WINDOW (F)
   Window size = k (constant). Slide, track max sum.

B: Longest substring no repeats → VARIABLE WINDOW (V)
   Window size varies. Expand right, shrink left on duplicate.

C: Duplicate within k positions → FIXED WINDOW (F) with HashSet
   Keep a sliding HashSet of last k elements.
   Add new, remove element k positions back, check for duplicate.

D: Min length subarray sum ≥ target → VARIABLE WINDOW (V)
   Expand right until sum ≥ target, record length, shrink left.
   Window shrinks when condition MET (opposite of usual).

E: Sort the array → NEITHER (N)
   Sorting is not a subarray/window problem.

test_cases.expected:
A: Max sum exactly k consecutive → FIXED WINDOW (F)
   Window size = k (constant). Slide, track max sum.

B: Longest substring no repeats → VARIABLE WINDOW (V)
   Window size varies. Expand right, shrink left on duplicate.

C: Duplicate within k positions → FIXED WINDOW (F) with HashSet
   Keep a sliding HashSet of last k elements.
   Add new, remove element k positions back, check for duplicate.

D: Min length subarray sum ≥ target → VARIABLE WINDOW (V)
   Expand right until sum ≥ target, record length, shrink left.
   Window shrinks when condition MET (opposite of usual).

E: Sort the array → NEITHER (N)
   Sorting is not a subarray/window problem.

hints: Fixed window: problem states exact window size k
Variable window: optimize window size subject to some constraint
Min subarray sum: window shrinks when condition satisfied (different direction)

exercises.title: Maximum Sum Subarray of Size K
exercises.instructions:
Find maximum sum of any k consecutive elements.
Example: nums=[2,3,4,1,5], k=2 → 9 (subarray [4,5])
O(n) sliding window.
hints: Build first window: sum of nums[0..k-1]
Slide: windowSum += nums[i] - nums[i-k]
Track max throughout sliding

exercises.title: Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters (LeetCode #3)
exercises.instructions:
Find length of longest substring without repeating characters.
Example: 'abcabcbb' → 3, 'bbbbb' → 1, 'pwwkew' → 3
O(n) sliding window with HashSet.
hints: HashSet tracks chars currently in window
Shrink left until duplicate removed
hi - lo + 1: current window size

exercises.title: Find All Anagrams in a String (LeetCode #438)
exercises.instructions:
Given s and p, return start indices of p's anagrams in s.
Example: s='cbaebabacd', p='abc' → [0,6]

Fixed window of size p.length(). Compare char frequencies.
O(n) sliding window with frequency array.
hints: int[26] frequency array: O(1) comparison vs O(k) per position
Slide: add chars[i], remove chars[i-k], compare
Arrays.equals(need,have): O(26) = O(1) — constant charset

exercises.title: Minimum Size Subarray Sum (LeetCode #209)
exercises.instructions:
Find minimum length of contiguous subarray with sum ≥ target. Return 0 if none.
Example: nums=[2,3,1,2,4,3], target=7 → 2 ([4,3])

Variable window: expand until sum ≥ target, then shrink from left.
hints: Shrink when condition MET (not when invalid) — opposite of usual pattern
Record length before shrinking: we want minimum length with sum >= target
Integer.MAX_VALUE sentinel: return 0 if never satisfied

exercises.title: Longest Substring with At Most 2 Distinct Characters
exercises.instructions:
Find length of longest substring containing at most 2 distinct characters.
Example: 'eceba' → 3 ('ece'), 'ccaabbb' → 5 ('aabbb')
Variable window with HashMap counting frequency.
hints: freq.size() > k: window invalid (too many distinct chars)
Remove from map when frequency hits 0
Parameterize k: same code works for 'at most k distinct'

exercises.title: Longest Substring with Same Letters After K Replacements (LeetCode #424)
exercises.instructions:
Given string s and integer k, find length of longest substring where you can replace at most k characters to make all letters the same.

Example: s='AABABBA', k=1 → 4

Key insight: window is valid if (windowSize - maxFreqInWindow) <= k
hints: (windowSize - maxFreqChar) = replacements needed in window
maxFreq may be stale after shrink — ok since we only grow the answer
Window shrinks by exactly 1 when invalid (not a while loop here)

exercises.title: Contains Duplicate II (LeetCode #219) — window with HashSet
exercises.instructions:
Given array and k, return true if there are two distinct indices i,j where nums[i]==nums[j] and |i-j|<=k.

Example: nums=[1,2,3,1], k=3 → true

Fixed sliding window of size k using HashSet.
hints: Fixed window size k: HashSet holds last k elements
Check duplicate BEFORE adding (then add)
Remove element k positions back: nums[i-k]

exercises.title: Why sliding window is O(n) despite nested loops
exercises.expected_solution:
PROOF that this is O(n), not O(n²):

Key observation: lo only moves FORWARD, never backward.

Total lo++ operations across the entire execution:
  lo starts at 0, ends at most at n-1
  Total lo increments ≤ n (it can only go up, never down)

Total hi++ operations: exactly n (outer for loop)

Total operations = hi movements + lo movements
                 = n + ≤n
                 = O(n)

This is called AMORTIZED analysis:
  The while loop might run many times for ONE hi
  But averaged over all n iterations, it runs ≤ 1 time per hi
  Because lo can only move n total steps

Analogie: imagine two people walking a hallway n meters long
  hi walks from one end to the other: n steps
  lo follows behind, never passing hi: ≤ n steps
  Total steps: ≤ 2n = O(n)

The two pointers approach to proving O(n):
  Count total movements of lo + hi
  Each pointer is monotonically non-decreasing → each moves at most n times

test_cases.expected:
PROOF that this is O(n), not O(n²):

Key observation: lo only moves FORWARD, never backward.

Total lo++ operations across the entire execution:
  lo starts at 0, ends at most at n-1
  Total lo increments ≤ n (it can only go up, never down)

Total hi++ operations: exactly n (outer for loop)

Total operations = hi movements + lo movements
                 = n + ≤n
                 = O(n)

This is called AMORTIZED analysis:
  The while loop might run many times for ONE hi
  But averaged over all n iterations, it runs ≤ 1 time per hi
  Because lo can only move n total steps

Analogie: imagine two people walking a hallway n meters long
  hi walks from one end to the other: n steps
  lo follows behind, never passing hi: ≤ n steps
  Total steps: ≤ 2n = O(n)

The two pointers approach to proving O(n):
  Count total movements of lo + hi
  Each pointer is monotonically non-decreasing → each moves at most n times

hints: lo only moves forward: count total lo++ across all iterations
Total hi++ = n. Total lo++ ≤ n. Total = O(n).
This is amortized analysis of two pointer movements

exercises.title: Subarray Sum Equals K (LeetCode #560)
exercises.instructions:
Count number of contiguous subarrays with sum equal to k.
Example: nums=[1,1,1], k=2 → 2
Example: nums=[1,2,3], k=3 → 2 ([1,2] and [3])

Sliding window doesn't work directly (negative numbers possible!).
Use prefix sum + HashMap: O(n).
hints: prefix[j] - prefix[i] = k → need prefix[i] = prefix[j] - k
HashMap stores count of each prefix sum seen
Initialize map with {0:1}: handles subarrays starting from index 0

exercises.title: Fruit Into Baskets (LeetCode #904) — at most 2 types
exercises.instructions:
You have 2 baskets. Each basket holds exactly 1 type of fruit. Find maximum number of fruits you can collect from a contiguous subarray using at most 2 basket types.

Example: fruits=[1,2,1] → 3 (all), fruits=[0,1,2,2] → 3 ([1,2,2])

This is 'longest subarray with at most 2 distinct values' — sliding window.
hints: basket.size() > 2: window invalid (more than 2 fruit types)
Remove from basket when count hits 0
Same pattern as atMost2Distinct — just with array values instead of chars

exercises.title: Maximum Points on a Line of Students (Sliding Window on Circular)
exercises.instructions:
Zerodha trading floor: students sit in a circle. Pick at most 2k seats from the two ends of a row of n seats. Find maximum score.

scores=[1,2,3,4,5], k=2 → take 4 from ends: [5,4,1,2]=12 or [1,2,4,5]=12

This is equivalent to: max sum of k elements from left + right ends.
Solution: find MINIMUM sum subarray of size (n-k) in middle → answer = totalSum - minMiddle.
hints: Equivalent: find min sum of (n-k) contiguous middle elements
total - minMiddle = max score from k elements at ends
Fixed window size = n-k

exercises.title: Longest Subarray of 1s After Deleting One Element (LeetCode #1493)
exercises.instructions:
Given binary array, delete ONE element. Return length of longest subarray containing only 1s after deletion.

Example: nums=[1,1,0,1] → 3 (delete the 0)
Example: nums=[0,1,1,1,0,1,1,0,1] → 5

Sliding window: allow at most 1 zero in window.
hints: At most 1 zero in window (the one we delete)
Answer = hi - lo (not hi-lo+1): always delete exactly 1 element
nums=[1,1,1]: window of 3, delete 1 → length 2

exercises.title: Complete M30 T2 Sliding Window Synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build the complete sliding window toolkit:
1. maxSumFixedWindow(nums, k) — fixed window max sum
2. longestNoRepeat(s) — variable window, HashSet
3. minSubarraySum(target, nums) — min length with sum ≥ target
4. findAnagrams(s, p) — fixed window with freq array
5. All 5 tests pass
hints: Fixed window: sum += nums[i] - nums[i-k]
Variable: expand right, shrink left while invalid
minSubarraySum: shrink while sum >= target (not while invalid)
